g.data-narration: Welcome to Logistic Regression! Today we'll explore this fundamental classification algorithm that powers many real-world applications.
g.text: A Complete Visual Guide for Machine Learning

g.data-narration: Logistic regression is a classification algorithm despite its name. It predicts the probability that an input belongs to a particular class using the sigmoid function.
g.text: • Applications: Spam detection, Medical diagnosis, Credit risk

g.data-narration: Linear regression can predict values outside [0,1], which makes no sense for probabilities. Logistic regression solves this with the sigmoid function.
g.text: • Linear regression: predictions can be negative or >1

g.data-narration: The sigmoid (logistic) function σ(z) = 1/(1+e^(-z)) transforms any real number into a value between 0 and 1, perfect for probabilities.
The sigmoid curve shows how input values map to probabilities. Notice how it approaches 0 for negative inputs and 1 for large positive inputs.
The logistic regression model computes a weighted sum of features and passes it through the sigmoid to get a probability of class 1.
g.text: If P ≥ 0.5 → predict SPAM, else NOT SPAM

g.data-narration: The default threshold is 0.5: predict class 1 if probability is at least 50%, otherwise class 0. This threshold can be adjusted for specific needs.
g.text: Adjust threshold based on cost of errors:
• Higher threshold → fewer false positives, more false negatives
• Lower threshold → more false positives, fewer false negatives

g.data-narration: Odds represent the ratio of probability of success to failure. Log-odds is the natural log of odds, which logistic regression actually models as linear!
Each coefficient represents the change in log-odds for a one-unit increase in the feature. The odds ratio e^(bᵢ) shows how odds multiply.
g.text: = factor odds change for 1-unit X increase
Example: If b = 0.5 for "has_spam_words", then OR = e^0.5 ≈ 1.65 → odds of spam increase by 65% per spam word!

g.data-narration: Log-loss (binary cross-entropy) is the cost function. It heavily penalizes confident wrong predictions, making optimization reliable and convex.
Gradient descent iteratively updates coefficients to minimize log-loss. The gradient shows the direction to move for the steepest increase in loss.
g.text: No closed-form solution like linear regression; must use iterative optimization. Loss surface is convex, guaranteeing global minimum.

g.data-narration: The decision boundary is where probability equals 0.5, which occurs when the linear combination equals zero - a linear hyperplane!
g.text: Decision boundary is always LINEAR in feature space!

g.data-narration: For multiple classes, use One-vs-Rest (K binary classifiers) or Multinomial/Softmax (direct K-class model with probabilities summing to 1).
g.text: • Train K binary classifiers (one per class)

g.data-narration: Regularization prevents overfitting by adding penalty terms. L2 shrinks coefficients, while L1 can drive them to zero for feature selection.
Key metrics include accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, and AUC-ROC. Choose based on class balance and error costs.
In summary: logistic regression uses sigmoid to output probabilities, is optimized with log-loss, and produces linear decision boundaries.
g.text: • Classification algorithm despite name → outputs probabilities via sigmoid
• Models log-odds as linear function of features → coefficients interpretable as odds ratios
• Log-loss is convex → guaranteed global minimum

g.data-narration: For exams: memorize sigmoid formula, know log-loss derivation, understand odds ratios, and remember the linear decision boundary!
